Lab 1: The Apollo Missions¶

Introduction¶

Imagine you are an engineer working on the very early days of the Apollo program. In order to send people to the moon, you need a basic understanding of the gravitational potential and forces the mission will experience. You also need to start quantifying the performance of the new rocket that will carry the capsule: the Saturn V.

Your team at NASA has been tasked with conducting a series of investigations, which you are guided through below. You will make some plots, and also write some short paragraphs with your scientific conclusions. The NASA Director Gene Kranz will use your notebook to defend NASA funding to congress. So your answers should be short and to the point, quantitative in nature, and contain highly polished plots, with appropriate titles, units, etc.

Other important requirements:

  • Your final notebook---BOTH the .ipynb file AND the html export---should be COMMITTED and PUSHED to your fork of the PHYS265-spring26 GitHub repository. Instructions for doing this are available online.
  • All plotting should be done in object oriented fashion, as discussed in class. Any use of “plt.plot()” will result in zero points being awarded for that question.
  • Before submitting your work, reset JLD and then re-run your final code (Kernel → Restart Kernel and Run All Cells…) Make sure your code executes as intended and without error. The first cell should be "1", and the cells should execute in order.

A table of relevant physical constants is below. Good luck!

Quantity Value
Gravitational Constant ($G$) $6.67 \times 10^{-11}$ m³/kg/s²
Gravitational Acceleration ($g$) 9.81 m/s²
Mass of the Earth ($M_E$) $5.9 \times 10^{24}$ kg
Mass of the Moon ($M_M$) $7.3 \times 10^{22}$ kg
Mass of Apollo Command Module 5500 kg
Radius of the Earth ($R_E$) 6378 km
Radius of the Moon ($R_M$) 1737 km
Distance from Earth to Moon ($d_{EM}$) $3.8 \times 10^{8}$ m
Exhaust Velocity of Saturn V Stage 1 (S-1C) ($v_e$) $2.4 \times 10^{3}$ m/s
Burn Rate of S-1C ($\dot{m}$) $1.3 \times 10^{4}$ kg/s
Wet Mass of S-1C ($m_0$) $2.8 \times 10^{6}$ kg
Dry Mass of S-1C ($m_f$) $7.5 \times 10^5$ kg
In [1]:
#show
# In the code cell beneath this one you can put your imports,
# and define any variables you might need (e.g. the mass of the earth)
In [2]:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt  
from matplotlib import colors 
from matplotlib.colors import LogNorm

G = 6.67 * 10**-11 
d_EM = 3.8 * 10**8
M_E = 5.9 * 10**24 
M_M = 7.3 * 10**22 
R_E = 6378 
R_M = 1373

Part 1: The Gravitational Potential of the Earth¶

The gravitational potential at a distance $r$ from a mass $M$ is given by:

$$\Phi(r) = -\frac{GM}{r}$$

where G is the gravitational constant.

  1. Code up a function that takes as an input the mass of a body $M$ , the cartesian location of the mass $(x_M , y_M)$, and the location of some distant evaluation point $(x, y)$. Have the function return the potential at the evaluation point. You may consider approximating the Earth as a point particle, but in which case, be careful! For in that approximation, the the potential contains a singularity. In our function, use all modern best practices for coding. E.g. type hints, type checks, pre and post conditions, doc strings, etc.
In [3]:
def grav_pot_earth(M: float, x_m: float, y_m: float, x: float, y: float): 
    #Define the position coordinates:
    mass_pos = (x_M, y_M)
    eval_pos = (x, y)

    #Define r using .norm to get distance 'r'
    r = np.linalg.norm(eval_pos - mass_pos) 
    
    if r == 0:
        return float('-inf') 

    potential = -G * M_E/r 
    return potential
  1. Make a 1D plot of the absolute value of the gravitational potential $|\Phi|$ as a function of distance $x$ from the surface of the Earth, assuming $y = 0$. Plot out to $1.5\times$ the distance from the Earth to the Moon. Because the potential falls quickly with r, make the y-axis logarithmic to visualize it better.
In [4]:
#def r values
R_start = R_E 
R_end = 1.5 * d_EM
    
R_values = np.linspace(R_start, R_end, 1000) 
#remember, the last number indicates how many "points" there are. The more points, the smoother the line

#Def v_abs function |V| = G * m/r
V_abs = np.abs((-G * M_E)/R_values) 

#Begin plotting!
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10,6)) 

ax.plot(R_values, V_abs, color='green', linewidth=1.5, label='Gravitational Potential of the Earth') 
ax.set_yscale('log') 

#Title and Label
ax.set_title("Absolute Gravitational Potential vs. Distance from Earth's Surface", fontsize = 20)
ax.set_xlabel("Distance from the Earth's Center [m]", fontsize=14) 
ax.set_ylabel("Absolute Potential |V| (J/kg)", fontsize = 14)  

ax.legend() 

#Adjust layout
ax.grid(True, which="both", linewidth=0.5, color='gray', alpha=0.35) #alpha adjusts opacity, true/both adjusts the axis scales to both be log
plt.tight_layout()
  1. Then, make a 2D color-mesh plot of the potential $\Phi$ with the Earth at the origin. Plot $|\Phi|$ over a large range of $x$ and $y$, for example, from $-1.5d_{EM}$ to $+1.5d_{EM}$ . Force the plot to have a square aspect ratio. Make sure to add a nice colorbar, and make the colorbar logarithmic.
In [5]:
#1D data
R_values_1D = np.linspace(R_start, R_end, 1000) 
V_abs_1d = np.abs(-G * M_E)/R_values 

#2D limits/axis data
lim = 1.5*d_EM 
x = np.linspace(-lim, lim, 500)
y = np.linspace(-lim, lim, 500) 
X, Y = np.meshgrid (x, y) 

#2D data 
R_2D = np.sqrt(X**2 + Y**2) #distance formula 
R_2D = np.clip(R_2D, R_E, None) #to prevent clipping 
V_abs_2D = abs(G*M_E)/R_2D 

#2D plotting 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10,8))

mesh = ax.pcolormesh(X, Y, V_abs_2D, cmap='viridis', shading='auto', norm = LogNorm()) 
ax.set_aspect("equal")

#title and label everything 
ax.set_title("2D Earth Gravitational Potential Map") 
ax.set_xlabel("x [m]") 
ax.set_ylabel("y [m]") 

#create the color bar 
cbar = fig.colorbar(mesh, ax=ax) 
cbar.set_label("Potential [J/kg]",fontsize=14) 

plt.tight_layout() 
plt.show()

Part 2: The Gravitational Potential of the Earth-Moon System¶

Now, imagine the Earth is at the origin $(x_{E},y_{E}) = (0,0)$, and the moon is at a location $(x_{M},y_{M}) = \big( \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}} \big)$.

  1. Make an updated 2D color-mesh plot, from $-1.5d_{EM}$ to $+1.5d_{EM}$, of the gravitational potential of the combined Earth-Moon system.
In [6]:
#2D limits/axis data
x = np.linspace(-1.5*d_EM, 1.5*d_EM, 500)
y = np.linspace(-1.5*d_EM, 1.5*d_EM, 500) 
X, Y = np.meshgrid (x, y)  

#set positions of earth and moon 
xe, ye = (0,0) 
xm, ym = (d_EM/np.sqrt(2), d_EM/np.sqrt(2))

#define distances from earth to moon 
r_e = np.sqrt((X - xe)**2 + (Y - ye)**2) #distance formula 
r_m = np.sqrt((X - xm)**2 + (Y - ym)**2) 

#prevent any clipping 
r_e = np.clip(r_e, R_E, None) 
r_m = np.clip(r_m, R_M, None)

#define earth and moon and total potentials 
Ve = -G * M_E / r_e 
Vm = -G * M_M / r_m 
Vtot = Ve + Vm 
abs_Vtot = np.abs(Vtot) 

#plotting
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10,6)) 

mesh1 = ax.pcolormesh(X, Y, abs_Vtot, norm=colors.LogNorm(vmin=abs_Vtot.min(), vmax=abs_Vtot.max()), cmap='viridis', shading='auto') 

#Label and Title: 
ax.set_title("2D Earth-Moon Gravitational Potential Map") 
ax.set_xlabel("x [m]") 
ax.set_ylabel("y [m]") 

cbar = fig.colorbar(mesh1, ax=ax) 
cbar.set_label("Potential magnitude [-V]",fontsize=14)
ax.set_aspect("equal")

plt.tight_layout() 
plt.show()
  1. Additionally, make a 2D contour plot over the same $x$ and $y$ range. Do not let all the contours bunch up around the Earth and Moon. You will probably again need to tell matplotlib to use a logarithmic color scale.
In [7]:
#2D limits/axis data
x = np.linspace(-1.5*d_EM, 1.5*d_EM, 500)
y = np.linspace(-1.5*d_EM, 1.5*d_EM, 500) 
X, Y = np.meshgrid (x, y)  

#set positions of earth and moon 
xe, ye = (0,0) 
xm, ym = (d_EM/np.sqrt(2), d_EM/np.sqrt(2))

#define distances from earth to moon 
r_e = np.sqrt((X - xe)**2 + (Y - ye)**2) #distance formula 
r_m = np.sqrt((X - xm)**2 + (Y - ym)**2) 

#prevent any clipping 
r_e = np.clip(r_e, R_E, None) 
r_m = np.clip(r_m, R_M, None)

#define earth and moon and total potentials 
Ve = -G * M_E / r_e 
Vm = -G * M_M / r_m 
Vtot = Ve + Vm 
abs_Vtot = np.abs(Vtot) 

#plotting
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10,6)) 

mesh1 = ax.pcolormesh(X, Y, abs_Vtot, norm=colors.LogNorm(vmin=abs_Vtot.min(), vmax=abs_Vtot.max()), cmap='viridis', shading='auto') 

levs = np.logspace(np.log10(abs_Vtot.min()), np.log10(abs_Vtot.max()), 15) 

#create contour plot
contours = ax.contour(X, Y, abs_Vtot, levels=levs, norm=colors.LogNorm(), colors = 'white', alpha = 0.5)
ax.clabel(contours, inline=True, fontsize = 8) 

#label and title
ax.set_title("2D Earth-Moon Gravitational Contours") 
ax.set_xlabel("x [m]") 
ax.set_ylabel("y [m]") 

cbar = fig.colorbar(mesh1, ax=ax) 
cbar.set_label("Potential magnitude [-V]",fontsize=14)

ax.set_aspect("equal") 

plt.tight_layout() 
plt.show()

Part 3: The Gravitational Force Field of the Earth-Moon System¶

The gravitational force $\vec{F}$ that a mass $M_1$ exerts on a mass $m_2$ is:

$$\vec{F}_{21} = -G \frac{M_1 m_2}{|\vec{r}_{21}|^2} \hat{r}_{21}$$

where $\vec{r}_{21}$ is the displacement vector from $M_1$ to $m_2$.

  1. Code up a function that takes as an input the mass of a body $M_1$, the mass of a secondary body $m_2$, the cartesian location of the first mass $(x_1, y_1)$, and the cartesian location of the second body $(x_2, y_2)$. Have the function return the two gravitational force components, $F_x$ and $F_y$, experienced by the mass $m_2$ from the force exerted by $M_1$. Note that the gravitational force contains a singularity, and so if you made the approximation that the Earth is a finite point, you will need to avoid it. Like for the 1D potential, make sure it follows modern best-coding practices.
In [8]:
def grav_force(M1: float, M2: float, x1: float, y1: float, x2: float, y2: float): 
    #x1, y1 = position of body 1, x2, y2 = position of body 2, M1 = mass of 1, m2 = mass of 2 
    #deifne position coordinates as arrays
    rx =x2-x1 
    ry=y2-y1 


    #calculate displacement vectors 
    R = np.sqrt(rx**2 + ry**2) 

    R = np.where(R==0, np.inf, R) 

    F_total = -G * M1 * M2 / (R**2) 

    Fx = F_total * (rx / R) 
    Fy = F_total * (ry / R) 

    return Fx, Fy
  1. Assuming the Earth is still at the origin and the Moon is still at $(x_M, y_M) = \big( \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{d_{EM}}{\sqrt{2}} \big)$, make a 2D streamplot of the gravitational force the Earth-Moon system will exert on the Apollo 11 command module. Plot the force over the same x-y range of $-1.5 d_{EM}$ to $+1.5 d_{EM}$. Add a nice color bar, which will again probably need to be logarithmic. Mark the location of the earth with a black circle, and the moon with a black square. Add a legend.
In [9]:
lim = 1.5 * d_EM 
x = np.linspace(-lim, lim, 100)
y = np.linspace(-lim, lim, 100) 
X, Y = np.meshgrid (x, y)  

m_apollo = 5500 #apollo mass 

#define force frmo moon 
xm, ym = d_EM/np.sqrt(2), d_EM/np.sqrt(2) 
Fx_E, Fy_E = grav_force(M_E, m_apollo, 0, 0 , X, Y) 
Fx_M, Fy_M = grav_force(M_M, m_apollo, xm, ym, X, Y)

#calculate forces            
Fx_total = Fx_E + Fx_M 
Fy_total = Fy_E + Fy_M 
F_mag = np.sqrt(Fx_total**2 + Fy_total**2) 

#plotting 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10,6)) 

#streamplot where the colors represent the magnitude of force 
stream = ax.streamplot(X, Y, Fx_total, Fy_total, color=F_mag, cmap='viridis', norm=colors.LogNorm(), linewidth=1.5) 

#mark moon and earhh 
ax.plot(0, 0, 'ko', markersize=8, label='Earth (Center)') 
ax.plot(xm, ym, 'ks', markersize=6, label='Moon (Location)') 

#labels and titles
ax.set_aspect("equal") 
ax.set_title("Gravitational Force Field of Earth-Moon System") 
ax.set_xlabel("x [m]") 
ax.set_ylabel("y [m]") 

#colorbar and legend 
cbar = fig.colorbar(stream.lines, ax=ax) 
cbar.set_label("Force Magnitude [N]", fontsize = 14) 
ax.legend(loc='upper left') 

plt.tight_layout() 
plt.show()
  1. You will note there is a point between the Earth and the Moon where the field lines seem to vanish. This is the point where the Earth's and Moon's gravitational forces cancel eachother. By setting $|\vec{F}_{moon}| = |\vec{F}_{earth}|$, calculate the radial distance of the "balance point." From there, identify the $x$ and $y$ coordinates of the balance point. Typeset your derivation (show multiple steps!) in latex in a markdown cell below. Then, calculate it numerically, and draw the answer onto a new version of your force field plot. Again mark the location of the earth with a black circle, and the moon with a black square; mark the location of the balance point with a red x. Add a legend.

Your Derivation Goes in the markdown cell below:

  1. Cancel $G$ and the mass of the spacecraft $m$: $$\frac{M_E}{R_E^2} = \frac{M_M}{R_M^2}$$
  2. Since the point is between the bodies, $R_M = d_{EM}-R_E$: $$\frac{M_E}{R_E^2} = \frac{M_M}{(d_{EM}-R_E)^2}$$
  3. Take the square root of both sides and rearrange to find $R_E$: $$\frac{\sqrt{M_E}}{R_E} = \frac{\sqrt{M_M}}{d_{EM}-R_E} \implies R_E = d_{EM} \left( \frac{\sqrt{M_E}}{\sqrt{M_E} + \sqrt{M_M}} \right)$$ Where $R_{E}$ is the radial distance from the center of the Earth to the balance point!

And your updated plot, including the balance point, goes in the cell below.

In [10]:
re_mag = d_EM * (np.sqrt(M_E)) / (np.sqrt(M_E) +np.sqrt(M_M)) 

x_bal = y_bal = re_mag/np.sqrt(2) 

lim = 1.5 * d_EM 
x_grid = np.linspace(-lim, lim, 100) 
y_grid = np.linspace(-lim, lim, 100) 
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x_grid, y_grid) 

#define forces
xm, ym = d_EM/np.sqrt(2), d_EM/np.sqrt(2) 
Fx_E, Fy_E = grav_force(M_E, m_apollo, 0, 0, X, Y) 
Fx_M, Fy_M = grav_force(M_M, m_apollo, xm, ym, X, Y)

Fx_tot = Fx_E + Fx_M 
Fy_tot = Fy_E + Fy_M 
F_mag = np.sqrt(Fx_tot**2 + Fy_tot**2) 

#plotting 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10,6)) 
stream = ax.streamplot(X, Y, Fx_tot, Fy_tot, color = F_mag, cmap = 'viridis', norm=colors.LogNorm(), linewidth=1.5) 

#add markers 
ax.plot(0,0, 'ko', markersize=8, label = 'Earth') 
ax.plot(xm, ym, 'ks', markersize=6, label='Moon') 
ax.plot(x_bal, y_bal, 'rx', markersize=8, label= 'Balance Point [L1]', markeredgewidth=3)
#label and title 
ax.set_aspect('equal')
ax.set_title("Force FIeld and Gravitational Balance Point of Earth-Moon System", fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel("x [m]")
ax.set_ylabel("y [m]")

cbar = fig.colorbar(stream.lines, ax=ax) 
cbar.set_label("Force Magnitude [N]") 
ax.legend(loc='upper left') 
plt.tight_layout()  
plt.show() 

#define balance point
print(f"The balance point is {re_mag:.2e} [m] from Earth!") 

The balance point is 3.42e+08 [m] from Earth!
  1. Quantitatively discuss your conclusions. Is the balance point close to the Moon or Earth? How close? Does this make sense?

Your Answer Goes in the markdown cell below:

The balance point is very close to the Moon compared with the Earth. The distance $R_E$ is 3.42E8 m from the center of the earth, and we are given a distance $d_{EM}$ which is 3.8E8. On top of this, $M_{E}$ is approximately $81*M_{M}$ and because Force is directly proportional to $\frac{M}{R^2}$.

Bearing these facts in mind, we know the force ratio is determined by $\sqrt{M_{E}}:\sqrt{M_{M}}$. This means the space craft must be 9 times further from Earth than the Moon to feel an equivalent force!

To balance the pull of something like the Earth, whose mass allows for an extremely large gravitational pull, you must be extremely close to a small mass. A smaller mass with have less of a gravitational pull compared to the Earth, so it is logical that you must be farther away from the Earth to also feel less of a pull.

Part 4: Altitude of the Saturn V Rocket¶

Rockets function by conservation of momentum. The ejection of fuel backwards propels the rocket forwards. The change in a rocket's velocity $\Delta v$ as a function of time $t$ (under a set of simplifying assumptions) is given by the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation:

$$\Delta v(t) = v_e \ln \left(\frac{m_0}{m(t)}\right) - gt$$

where $m_0$ is the initial "wet" mass (fuel + rocket parts + payload), $m(t) = m_0 - \dot{m}t$ is the mass at time $t$, $\dot{m}$ is the fuel burn rate (which we will assume to be constant), $v_e$ is the fuel exhaust velocity, and $g$ is the gravitational acceleration.

You can find the altitude $h$ of the rocket at "burnout" (when all the fuel is used up) by integrating the velocity over time:

$$h = \int_{0}^{T} \Delta v(t) \, dt$$

where $T$ is the total burn time of the rocket, which by conservation of momentum arguments is:

$$T = \frac{m_0 - m_f}{\dot{m}}$$

where $m_f$ is the final "dry mass" of the rocket, once all the fuel is burned.

In [11]:
#Constants: 
m_w = 2.97e6 #mass wet
m_d = 0.81e6 #mass dry
f_burn = 12900 
ve = 2580 
g = 9.81
  1. Calculate the burn time $T$ for the first stage of the new Saturn V rocket that NASA is planning to use to carry the Apollo capsule.
In [12]:
m_fuel = m_w-m_d 

T = m_fuel / f_burn 

print(f"Total Fuel Mass: {m_fuel:.2e} [kg]") 
print(f"Total Burn Time (T): {T:.2f} [s]") 

Total Fuel Mass: 2.16e+06 [kg]
Total Burn Time (T): 167.44 [s]
  1. Define a function that returns the change in velocity $\Delta v(t)$. It should take all necessary arguments, meaning: the time, wet mass, dry mass, burn rate, exhaust speed, and gravitational constant $g$. Have it return the change in velocity. Note that the rocket eventually runs out of fuel, so you should design your function carefully so that $\Delta v$ eventually becomes zero after all fuel is spent. Again, document and guard your function according to best practices.
In [13]:
def delta_v_calc(t: float, m_w:float, m_d: float, f_burn: float, ve: float, g: float): 
    T = (m_w-m_d)/ f_burn 

    if t > T or t < 0: 
        return 0.0 

    #mass at time t 
    mt = m_w - f_burn * t 

    delta_v = ve * np.log(m_w/mt) - (g*t) 

    return delta_v 

#test the function at t = 100 and 200 (before and after burnout) 
dv_100 = delta_v_calc(100, m_w, m_d, f_burn, ve, g) 
print(f"At t=100s, Delta v is {dv_100:.2f} [m/s]") 

dv_burn = delta_v_calc(200, m_w, m_d, f_burn, ve, g) #past burnout
print(f"At t=200s, Delta v is {dv_burn:.2f} [m/s]") 

At t=100s, Delta v is 489.00 [m/s]
At t=200s, Delta v is 0.00 [m/s]
  1. Compute the altitude of the rocket at the end of the burn. Do this by numerically integrating from $t=0$ (launch) to $t=T$ (burnout). Use the scipy.integrate.quad() function.
In [14]:
from scipy.integrate import quad 

#Variables:
T = (m_w-m_d)/ f_burn 

def delta_v_calc(t: float, m_w:float, m_d: float, f_burn: float, ve: float, g: float): 
    if t > T or t < 0: 
        return 0.0 

    #mass at time t: m(t)
    mt = m_w - f_burn * t 

    delta_v = ve * np.log(m_w/mt) - (g*t) 

    return delta_v 

#integrat using the quad(function, start, end, argument) scipy to find altitude: 
a_burnout, error = quad(delta_v_calc, 0, T, args=(m_w, m_d, f_burn, ve, g)) 

#print results 
print(f"Altitude at Burnout: {a_burnout:.2f} [m]") 

Altitude at Burnout: 83995.77 [m]
  1. Just last week, NASA got the test results back from the first prototype of Saturn V. They found that it burned for about 160 seconds and lifted the system to an altitude of about 70km. Director Kranz has asked you to compare your calculations for $T$ and $h$ to the testing data. You should offer some explanations about why your calculation is an over or under estimate, e.g. neglecting drag.

Your Answer Goes in the markdown cell below:

The model that we uesd predicted a higher performance than what was observed during the NASA prototype test. The burn time T was calculated $167.44$ [s], while NASA observed $160.00$ [s] (+7.44s difference). The Altitude was calculated as $83,995$ [m], while NASA observed $70,000.00$ [m] (+13.995.77m difference).

There are a few differences between the calculated values we made and the data NASA gathered. We made 3 assumptions for our model which affected burn time and altitude:

  1. Neglect of Atmospheric Drag
    Our altitude over-calculation is primarily due to our assumption that the rocket travels through an atmosphere where only gravity opposes motion, we did not account for the effects of drag on the final altitude. Saturn V will encounter air resistance at lower atmosphers, which acts as a resistant force against movement.

  2. Constant Fuel Burn Rate
    We assumed that the burn rate was constant, but this is not accurate. Prototypes may experience fluctuations in fuel, or cut off the engine early in order to preserve integrity.

  3. Constant Exhaust Velocity
    Lastly, we assumed that there was a constant exhaust velocity $v_e$. Rocket engines change with atmospheric pressure, and at lower altitudes the higher air pressure makes $v_e$ lower.

These assumptions lead to a miscalculation between the calculated data and the observed data, and shows that in order to make the most accurate calculations possible, you cannot neglect a single possible occurance.
